---
layout: post
title: "Logic and Proof Methods — The Language of Mathematics"
date: 2026-03-23
category: maths
lang: en
lang_pair: "/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-hindi/"
permalink: /blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/
description: "Master propositions, logical connectives, truth tables, quantifiers, and the four core proof methods — direct, contrapositive, contradiction, and induction."
---

<style>
  .math-post{font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.9;color:#2d2d2d;max-width:860px;margin:0 auto}
  .post-hero{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72 0%,#2a5298 50%,#7c3aed 100%);border-radius:16px;padding:38px 35px;color:white;margin-bottom:35px;position:relative;overflow:hidden}
  .post-hero::before{content:"⇒";position:absolute;right:20px;top:-10px;font-size:8rem;color:rgba(255,255,255,0.06);font-family:serif}
  .post-hero h1{font-size:1.85rem;font-weight:800;color:white;margin:0 0 10px;line-height:1.3}
  .post-hero p{font-size:1rem;opacity:.9;margin:0 0 14px}
  .lang-switch{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:8px;background:rgba(255,255,255,0.15);border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.35);border-radius:50px;padding:7px 18px;font-size:.85rem;color:white;text-decoration:none;transition:background .2s}
  .lang-switch:hover{background:rgba(255,255,255,0.28);color:white;text-decoration:none}
  .stats-strip{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);border-radius:14px;padding:22px 28px;display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;justify-content:space-around;gap:16px;margin-bottom:35px;text-align:center;color:white}
  .stat-item{display:flex;flex-direction:column;gap:4px;min-width:120px}
  .stat-val{font-size:1.6rem;font-weight:800}
  .stat-lbl{font-size:.78rem;opacity:.82;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.04em}
  .section-title{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:12px;margin:38px 0 18px;border-bottom:2px solid;border-image:linear-gradient(90deg,#1e3c72,transparent) 1;padding-bottom:6px}
  .sec-icon{width:38px;height:38px;border-radius:10px;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;font-size:1.15rem;flex-shrink:0}
  .section-title h2{font-size:1.35rem;font-weight:800;margin:0;background:linear-gradient(90deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);-webkit-background-clip:text;-webkit-text-fill-color:transparent}
  .info-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:22px 26px;margin:18px 0;box-shadow:0 2px 12px rgba(0,0,0,.08)}
  .info-card.purple{border-left:5px solid #7c3aed;background:#f5f3ff}
  .info-card.gold{border-left:5px solid #d97706;background:#fffbeb}
  .info-card.green{border-left:5px solid #059669;background:#ecfdf5}
  .info-card.hindi{border-left:5px solid #d97706;background:#fefce8;font-family:'Hind','Noto Sans Devanagari',sans-serif}
  .info-card-header{font-size:.75rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.08em;margin-bottom:10px}
  .info-card-header.purple{color:#7c3aed}
  .info-card-header.gold{color:#d97706}
  .info-card-header.green{color:#059669}
  .tt{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;margin:14px 0;font-size:.92rem}
  .tt th{background:#1e3c72;color:white;padding:9px 13px;text-align:center;font-size:.8rem}
  .tt td{padding:8px 13px;border-bottom:1px solid #e2e8f0;text-align:center}
  .tt tr:nth-child(even) td{background:#f8fafc}
  .proof-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:16px;margin:20px 0}
  .proof-card{background:white;border-radius:10px;padding:18px 20px;border-top:4px solid;box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,.07)}
  .proof-card h4{font-size:.88rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em;margin:0 0 8px}
  .proof-card p{margin:0;font-size:.92rem;line-height:1.65}
  .example-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:24px 26px;margin:22px 0;border-top:5px solid;box-shadow:0 2px 12px rgba(0,0,0,.08);transition:transform .2s,box-shadow .2s}
  .example-card:hover{transform:translateY(-2px);box-shadow:0 6px 22px rgba(0,0,0,.12)}
  .example-card.ex1{border-top-color:#059669}
  .example-card.ex2{border-top-color:#2a5298}
  .example-card.ex3{border-top-color:#7c3aed}
  .example-card.ex4{border-top-color:#dc2626}
  .example-header{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;margin-bottom:16px}
  .example-num{width:36px;height:36px;border-radius:50%;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;color:white;font-weight:800;font-size:1rem;flex-shrink:0}
  .ex1 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#059669,#34d399)}
  .ex2 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#2a5298,#60a5fa)}
  .ex3 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#7c3aed,#a78bfa)}
  .ex4 .example-num{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#dc2626,#f87171)}
  .example-label{font-size:.72rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.08em;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:20px}
  .ex1 .example-label{background:#d1fae5;color:#059669}
  .ex2 .example-label{background:#dbeafe;color:#2a5298}
  .ex3 .example-label{background:#ede9fe;color:#7c3aed}
  .ex4 .example-label{background:#fee2e2;color:#dc2626}
  .example-title{font-weight:700;font-size:1rem;color:#0f172a}
  .solution-block{background:#ecfdf5;border-left:4px solid #059669;border-radius:0 10px 10px 0;padding:16px 20px;margin-top:14px}
  .solution-block p{margin:6px 0}
  .warning-box{background:#fef2f2;border-left:5px solid #dc2626;border-radius:0 10px 10px 0;padding:20px 24px;margin:18px 0}
  .warning-box h4{color:#dc2626;margin:0 0 12px;font-size:1rem}
  .warning-item{margin:12px 0;padding-left:14px;border-left:2px solid #fca5a5}
  .warning-label{font-weight:700;color:#dc2626;font-size:.88rem}
  .revision-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(3,1fr);gap:18px;margin:22px 0}
  .rev-card{border-radius:12px;padding:20px 18px;color:white}
  .rev-card.a{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#2a5298)}
  .rev-card.b{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#7c3aed,#a78bfa)}
  .rev-card.c{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#d97706,#f59e0b)}
  .rev-card h4{font-size:.88rem;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em;margin:0 0 12px;opacity:.9}
  .rev-card ul{margin:0;padding-left:16px;font-size:.9rem;line-height:1.75}
  .app-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fit,minmax(185px,1fr));gap:16px;margin:22px 0}
  .app-card{background:white;border-radius:12px;padding:20px 18px;border-top:4px solid;box-shadow:0 2px 10px rgba(0,0,0,.07);transition:transform .2s}
  .app-card:hover{transform:translateY(-3px)}
  .app-card h4{font-size:.92rem;font-weight:700;margin:8px 0 6px;color:#0f172a}
  .app-card p{font-size:.85rem;color:#475569;margin:0;line-height:1.55}
  .app-icon{font-size:1.6rem}
  .summary-table{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;margin:18px 0;font-size:.92rem}
  .summary-table th{background:#1e3c72;color:white;padding:11px 14px;text-align:left;font-size:.82rem;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em}
  .summary-table td{padding:10px 14px;border-bottom:1px solid #e2e8f0;vertical-align:top}
  .summary-table tr:nth-child(even) td{background:#f8fafc}
  .highlight-result{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72,#7c3aed);border-radius:14px;padding:26px 30px;text-align:center;color:white;margin:28px 0}
  .highlight-result p{margin:0;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.75}
  .social-footer{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#0f172a,#1e3c72);color:white;border-radius:14px;padding:28px 30px;margin:40px 0 20px;text-align:center}
  .social-footer a{color:#93c5fd}
  .social-footer p{margin:8px 0}
  .pdf-section{text-align:center;margin:44px 0 28px}
  .pdf-btn{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1B2A4A,#2A5298);color:white;padding:15px 36px;border-radius:6px;font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1rem;font-weight:700;box-shadow:0 4px 18px rgba(27,42,74,.40);transition:transform .2s,box-shadow .2s;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn:hover{transform:translateY(-3px);box-shadow:0 8px 26px rgba(27,42,74,.50);color:white;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn .pi{font-size:1.5rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt{display:flex;flex-direction:column;text-align:left;line-height:1.35}
  .pdf-btn .pt1{font-size:1rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt2{font-size:.72rem;opacity:.82;font-weight:400}
  @media(max-width:700px){.proof-grid{grid-template-columns:1fr}.revision-grid{grid-template-columns:1fr}}
</style>

<div class="math-post">

<!-- §1 HERO + STATS -->
<div class="post-hero">
  <h1>Logic and Proof Methods</h1>
  <p>Every mathematical theorem — from a simple divisibility fact to the deepest result in analysis — rests on a rigorous logical argument. This post gives you the complete toolkit: the precise language of propositions and quantifiers, and the four proof strategies that between them cover every mathematical argument you will ever need to write.</p>
  <a class="lang-switch" href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-hindi/">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में पढ़ें</a>
</div>

<div class="stats-strip">
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">5</span><span class="stat-lbl">Connectives</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">∀ ∃</span><span class="stat-lbl">Quantifiers</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">4</span><span class="stat-lbl">Proof Methods</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">4</span><span class="stat-lbl">Solved Examples</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">CSIR/GATE/JAM</span><span class="stat-lbl">Exam Relevance</span></div>
</div>

<!-- §2 DEFINITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ede9fe;">&#x21D2;</div>
  <h2>Propositions, Connectives &amp; Quantifiers</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Proposition</div>
  <p>A <strong>proposition</strong> is a declarative sentence that is either <strong>true (T)</strong> or <strong>false (F)</strong>, but not both. Examples: "$2$ is prime" (T); "$\sqrt{2}\in\mathbb{Q}$" (F).</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; The Five Logical Connectives</div>
  <table class="tt">
    <thead><tr><th>Symbol</th><th>Name</th><th>Read as</th><th>False exactly when</th></tr></thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr><td>$\neg p$</td><td>Negation</td><td>not $p$</td><td>$p$ is true</td></tr>
      <tr><td>$p\wedge q$</td><td>Conjunction</td><td>$p$ and $q$</td><td>at least one false</td></tr>
      <tr><td>$p\vee q$</td><td>Disjunction</td><td>$p$ or $q$</td><td>both false</td></tr>
      <tr><td>$p\Rightarrow q$</td><td>Implication</td><td>if $p$ then $q$</td><td>$p$ true, $q$ false</td></tr>
      <tr><td>$p\Leftrightarrow q$</td><td>Biconditional</td><td>$p$ iff $q$</td><td>different truth values</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  <p><strong>Key equivalences:</strong> $\;p\Rightarrow q\equiv\neg q\Rightarrow\neg p\equiv\neg p\vee q\;$ &nbsp;|&nbsp; $\;\neg(p\Rightarrow q)\equiv p\wedge\neg q$</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Quantifiers and Their Negations</div>
  <p>Let $P(x)$ be a predicate with domain $D$.</p>
  <p><strong>Universal:</strong> $\forall x\in D,\;P(x)$ — true when $P(x)$ holds for <em>every</em> $x\in D$.</p>
  <p><strong>Existential:</strong> $\exists\,x\in D,\;P(x)$ — true when $P(x)$ holds for <em>at least one</em> $x\in D$.</p>
  <p><strong>Negation rules (De Morgan for quantifiers):</strong></p>
  <p>$$\neg\bigl(\forall x\in D,\;P(x)\bigr)\equiv\exists\,x\in D,\;\neg P(x) \qquad \neg\bigl(\exists\,x\in D,\;P(x)\bigr)\equiv\forall x\in D,\;\neg P(x)$$</p>
</div>

📖 <strong>Reference</strong>: Apostol, T.M., <strong>Mathematical Analysis</strong>, 2nd ed., Ch. 1, §1.1–1.2. Also: Rudin, W., <strong>Principles of Mathematical Analysis</strong>, 3rd ed., Ch. 1, §1.1.

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F517; Prerequisites</div>
  <p>Quantifiers range over sets, so set notation is assumed. Read first: <a href="/blog/sets-and-basic-notation-english/"><strong>Sets and Basic Notation — A Foundation for Mathematics</strong></a>.</p>
</div>

<!-- §3 INTUITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#d1fae5;">&#x1F4A1;</div>
  <h2>Intuition — The Four Proof Methods</h2>
</div>

<p>A <strong>proof</strong> is a watertight argument — every step must follow logically from what came before. These four strategies cover every theorem you will ever need to prove.</p>

<div class="proof-grid">
  <div class="proof-card" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
    <h4 style="color:#2a5298;">&#x2192; Direct Proof</h4>
    <p>Assume $p$, deduce $q$. The most natural approach — use when the hypothesis leads straight to the conclusion.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="proof-card" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
    <h4 style="color:#7c3aed;">&#x21D4; Contrapositive</h4>
    <p>Prove $\neg q\Rightarrow\neg p$ instead. Logically identical to direct proof, but sometimes $\neg q$ is an easier starting point.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="proof-card" style="border-top-color:#dc2626;">
    <h4 style="color:#dc2626;">&#x26A1; Contradiction</h4>
    <p>Assume $\neg S$, derive absurdity. Best for irrationality proofs, infinitude arguments, or "X cannot exist."</p>
  </div>
  <div class="proof-card" style="border-top-color:#059669;">
    <h4 style="color:#059669;">&#x1F501; Induction</h4>
    <p>Prove $P(1)$, then $P(k)\Rightarrow P(k+1)$. The standard method whenever the statement is indexed by $\mathbb{N}$.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="info-card hindi">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में संक्षेप</div>
  <p><strong>कथन (Proposition)</strong> वह वाक्य है जो सत्य या असत्य हो। पाँच तार्किक संयोजक: $\neg$ (Negation), $\wedge$ (Conjunction), $\vee$ (Disjunction), $\Rightarrow$ (Implication), $\Leftrightarrow$ (Biconditional)। <strong>परिमाणक:</strong> $\forall$ (सार्वत्रिक — सभी के लिए) और $\exists$ (अस्तित्व — कम से कम एक के लिए)। निषेध नियम: $\neg(\forall x\;P)\equiv\exists x\;\neg P$। चार Proof विधियाँ — Direct, Contrapositive, Contradiction और Mathematical Induction — मिलकर गणित का पूर्ण तर्क-आधार बनाती हैं।</p>
</div>

<!-- §4 SOLVED EXAMPLES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x270F;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Solved Examples</h2>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex1">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">1</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Very Easy &nbsp;|&nbsp; Truth Table</div>
      <div class="example-title">Show $(p\Rightarrow q)\wedge(q\Rightarrow p)\equiv p\Leftrightarrow q$ via truth table</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <table class="tt" style="margin:8px 0">
      <thead><tr><th>$p$</th><th>$q$</th><th>$p\Rightarrow q$</th><th>$q\Rightarrow p$</th><th>$(p\Rightarrow q)\wedge(q\Rightarrow p)$</th><th>$p\Leftrightarrow q$</th></tr></thead>
      <tbody>
        <tr><td>T</td><td>T</td><td>T</td><td>T</td><td><strong>T</strong></td><td>T</td></tr>
        <tr><td>T</td><td>F</td><td>F</td><td>T</td><td><strong>F</strong></td><td>F</td></tr>
        <tr><td>F</td><td>T</td><td>T</td><td>F</td><td><strong>F</strong></td><td>F</td></tr>
        <tr><td>F</td><td>F</td><td>T</td><td>T</td><td><strong>T</strong></td><td>T</td></tr>
      </tbody>
    </table>
    <p>Columns 5 and 6 are identical, confirming $(p\Rightarrow q)\wedge(q\Rightarrow p)\equiv p\Leftrightarrow q$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex2">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">2</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Easy–Medium &nbsp;|&nbsp; Negating Quantifiers</div>
      <div class="example-title">Negate the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition of a limit</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <p>Write the precise negation of: "For every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $\delta>0$ such that $\lvert x-a\rvert<\delta\Rightarrow\lvert f(x)-L\rvert<\varepsilon$."</p>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Original:</strong> $\forall\varepsilon>0\;\;\exists\,\delta>0\;\;P(\varepsilon,\delta)$.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 1</strong> — swap quantifiers (De Morgan twice):<br> $\exists\,\varepsilon>0\;\;\forall\delta>0\;\;\neg P(\varepsilon,\delta)$.</p>
    <p><strong>Step 2</strong> — negate implication $(\neg(A\Rightarrow B)\equiv A\wedge\neg B)$:<br> $\neg P\equiv\lvert x-a\rvert<\delta\;\wedge\;\lvert f(x)-L\rvert\geq\varepsilon$.</p>
    <p><strong>Full negation:</strong> "There exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that for every $\delta>0$, there is an $x$ with $\lvert x-a\rvert<\delta$ and $\lvert f(x)-L\rvert\geq\varepsilon$." (This is exactly $\lim_{x\to a}f(x)\neq L$.) $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex3">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">3</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Medium–Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; Mathematical Induction</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}k^2=\dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}$ for all $n\in\mathbb{N}$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Base case</strong> $(n=1)$: LHS $=1$. RHS $=\dfrac{1\cdot2\cdot3}{6}=1$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Inductive step:</strong> Assume $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{m}k^2=\dfrac{m(m+1)(2m+1)}{6}$. Then:</p>
    <p>$$\sum_{k=1}^{m+1}k^2=\frac{m(m+1)(2m+1)}{6}+(m+1)^2 =\frac{(m+1)\bigl[m(2m+1)+6(m+1)\bigr]}{6}$$</p>
    <p>$$=\frac{(m+1)(2m^2+7m+6)}{6}=\frac{(m+1)(m+2)(2m+3)}{6}$$</p>
    <p>which is the formula for $n=m+1$. By induction the result holds for all $n\in\mathbb{N}$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex4">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">4</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; CSIR NET / GATE / IIT JAM Level</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove $6\mid n^3-n$ for all $n\in\mathbb{N}$ — two independent proofs</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Method (i) — Induction.</strong></p>
    <p><em>Base</em> $(n=1)$: $1-1=0=6\cdot0$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><em>Step:</em> Assume $6\mid k^3-k$. Then $(k+1)^3-(k+1)=(k^3-k)+3k(k+1)$. By hypothesis $6\mid(k^3-k)$. Since one of $k,k+1$ is even, $2\mid k(k+1)$, so $6\mid3k(k+1)$. Hence $6\mid(k+1)^3-(k+1)$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Method (ii) — Direct factorisation.</strong></p>
    <p>$n^3-n=(n-1)\,n\,(n+1)$ — the product of three consecutive integers. Any three consecutive integers contain a multiple of $2$ and a multiple of $3$, so the product is divisible by $\text{lcm}(2,3)=6$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §5 QUICK REVISION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#dbeafe;">&#x26A1;</div>
  <h2>Quick Revision Cards</h2>
</div>

<div class="revision-grid">
  <div class="rev-card a">
    <h4>&#x1F4CA; A — Key Symbols &amp; Equivalences</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>$p\Rightarrow q\equiv\neg q\Rightarrow\neg p\equiv\neg p\vee q$</li>
      <li>$\neg(p\Rightarrow q)\equiv p\wedge\neg q$</li>
      <li>$p\Leftrightarrow q\equiv(p\Rightarrow q)\wedge(q\Rightarrow p)$</li>
      <li>$\neg(\forall x\;P)\equiv\exists x\;\neg P$</li>
      <li>$\neg(\exists x\;P)\equiv\forall x\;\neg P$</li>
      <li>$p\Rightarrow q$ vacuously true when $p$ is false</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card b">
    <h4>&#x2699;&#xFE0F; B — Proof Method Chooser</h4>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Direct:</strong> hypothesis → conclusion naturally</li>
      <li><strong>Contrapositive:</strong> easier to assume $\neg q$</li>
      <li><strong>Contradiction:</strong> irrationality, "cannot exist"</li>
      <li><strong>Induction:</strong> statement indexed by $\mathbb{N}$</li>
      <li>Contrapositive ≠ Converse!</li>
      <li>Induction requires BOTH base case AND step</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card c">
    <h4>&#x1F3AF; C — Exam Tips</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>&#x1F535; <strong>CSIR NET:</strong> Negate $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ def: swap quantifiers then negate implication</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E2; <strong>GATE:</strong> Always label "Base case" and "Inductive step" explicitly</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E0; <strong>IIT JAM:</strong> Contrapositive ≠ Converse — classic MCQ trap</li>
      <li>&#x1F534; <strong>B.Sc. Raj.:</strong> Write inductive hypothesis in full before the step</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §6 COMMON MISTAKES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fee2e2;">&#x26A0;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Common Mistakes</h2>
</div>

<div class="warning-box">
  <h4>&#x274C; Errors to Avoid</h4>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 1: Contrapositive vs. Converse</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "$p\Rightarrow q\equiv q\Rightarrow p$." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> The <em>contrapositive</em> $\neg q\Rightarrow\neg p$ is logically equivalent; the <em>converse</em> $q\Rightarrow p$ is NOT.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 2: Wrong negation of implication</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> $\neg(p\Rightarrow q)\equiv\neg p\Rightarrow\neg q$. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> $\neg(p\Rightarrow q)\equiv p\wedge\neg q$. Negating an implication gives a conjunction, never another implication.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 3: Omitting the base case in induction</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Proving only the inductive step. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> Both parts are mandatory. Without the base case the chain never starts. Classic failure: the "proof" that all horses are the same colour breaks at $n=2$.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 4: Wrong negation of quantifiers</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> $\neg(\forall x\;P(x))\equiv\forall x\;\neg P(x)$. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> $\neg(\forall x\;P(x))\equiv\exists\,x\;\neg P(x)$. The quantifier <em>flips</em> AND the predicate is negated — both changes are required.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §7 APPLICATIONS -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ecfdf5;">&#x1F310;</div>
  <h2>Real-World Applications</h2>
</div>

<div class="app-grid">
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F5A5;&#xFE0F;</div>
    <h4>Program Verification</h4>
    <p>Hoare logic formalises $\{P\}\;C\;\{Q\}$ — a direct implementation of implication. Loop invariant correctness proofs use mathematical induction.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#059669;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F512;</div>
    <h4>Cryptography (RSA)</h4>
    <p>RSA decryption correctness uses contradiction and the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. Every security proof in cryptography is a rigorous logical argument.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F916;</div>
    <h4>Automated Theorem Proving</h4>
    <p>Lean, Coq, and Isabelle encode exactly these connectives and quantifiers. Every proof step must cite an axiom or a previously verified lemma.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#d97706;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F5C4;&#xFE0F;</div>
    <h4>Database Query Optimisation</h4>
    <p>Rewriting SQL <code>NOT EXISTS</code> as <code>FOR ALL NOT</code> is De Morgan's law for quantifiers applied algorithmically to relational algebra.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §8 SUMMARY TABLE -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#e0e7ff;">&#x1F4CB;</div>
  <h2>Summary Table &amp; Key Result</h2>
</div>

<table class="summary-table">
  <thead>
    <tr><th>Concept</th><th>Statement / Formula</th><th>Condition</th><th>Reference</th></tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr><td><strong>Implication</strong></td><td>$p\Rightarrow q\equiv\neg p\vee q$</td><td>Any $p,q$</td><td>Apostol Ch. 1</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Contrapositive</strong></td><td>$p\Rightarrow q\equiv\neg q\Rightarrow\neg p$</td><td>Tautology</td><td>Apostol Ch. 1</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Neg. Quantifier</strong></td><td>$\neg(\forall x\;P)\equiv\exists\,x\;\neg P$</td><td>Any domain</td><td>Standard</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Induction</strong></td><td>Base $+$ Step $\Rightarrow\forall n\in\mathbb{N}$</td><td>$P(n)$ well-defined</td><td>Apostol §1.2</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Contradiction</strong></td><td>Assume $\neg S$, derive $r\wedge\neg r$</td><td>Any $S$</td><td>Standard</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Sum of squares</strong></td><td>$\sum_{k=1}^{n}k^2=\dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}$</td><td>$n\in\mathbb{N}$</td><td>By induction</td></tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<div class="highlight-result">
  <p><strong>The Four-Method Toolkit:</strong></p>
  <p>$$\text{Direct}\quad\big|\quad\text{Contrapositive}\quad\big|\quad\text{Contradiction}\quad\big|\quad\text{Induction}$$</p>
  <p>Every theorem in analysis, algebra, and topology is proved by one — or a combination — of these four strategies.</p>
</div>

<!-- §9 CROSS-REFERENCES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x1F517;</div>
  <h2>Cross-References &amp; Related Posts</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F4DA; Prerequisites &amp; Next Steps</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190; Prerequisite:</strong> <a href="/blog/sets-and-basic-notation-english/"><strong>Sets and Basic Notation — A Foundation for Mathematics</strong></a> — quantifiers range over sets; De Morgan's laws for sets and for quantifiers are parallel results built on the same logical foundation.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x2192; Next Topic:</strong> <em>Relations and Functions</em> — formally defined using set-builder notation and the logical quantifiers developed here.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x1F4D6; Further Reading:</strong> Apostol, Ch. 1, §§1.1–1.4; Rudin, Ch. 1, §§1.1–1.5; Bartle &amp; Sherbert, Ch. 1 &amp; Appendix.</p>
</div>
